_source: Real, verifiable football facts per nation (Wikipedia-grounded). Two per country, used by the Atlas 'Did you know' block. No fabricated content.

Argentina: Argentina have won the World Cup three times - 1978, 1986 and 2022.
Lionel Messi captained Argentina to the 2022 World Cup title in Qatar.
Spain: Spain won their first World Cup in 2010, beating the Netherlands in the final.
They won three straight majors: Euro 2008, the 2010 World Cup and Euro 2012.
France: France have won two World Cups - 1998 on home soil and 2018 in Russia.
Kylian Mbappé scored a hat-trick in the 2022 World Cup final, a rare feat.
England: England won their only World Cup in 1966, as hosts at Wembley.
Geoff Hurst is the only man to score a hat-trick in a World Cup final.
Portugal: Cristiano Ronaldo is Portugal's all-time leading scorer and most-capped player.
Portugal finished third at the 1966 World Cup, led by Eusébio's nine goals.
Brazil: Brazil are the only nation to have played in every FIFA World Cup.
They have won the World Cup a record five times.
Morocco: Morocco became the first African nation to reach a World Cup semi-final, in 2022.
In 1986 they were the first African side to top a World Cup group.
Netherlands: The Dutch reached three World Cup finals - 1974, 1978 and 2010 - without winning.
They pioneered 'Total Football' at the 1974 World Cup.
Belgium: Belgium finished third at the 2018 World Cup, their best-ever result.
Belgium's 'golden generation' topped the FIFA World Ranking from 2018.
Germany: Germany have won the World Cup four times, most recently in 2014.
Miroslav Klose is the World Cup's all-time top scorer with 16 goals.
Croatia: Croatia reached the World Cup final in 2018, losing to France.
Luka Modrić won the Golden Ball as the best player of the 2018 tournament.
Colombia: Colombia's James Rodríguez won the Golden Boot at the 2014 World Cup.
Goalkeeper René Higuita was famous for his audacious 'scorpion kick'.
Mexico: Mexico has hosted the World Cup twice, in 1970 and 1986.
Estadio Azteca staged both the 1970 and 1986 finals.
Senegal: Senegal beat holders France on their World Cup debut in 2002.
They won their first Africa Cup of Nations in 2021.
Uruguay: Uruguay won the very first World Cup, as hosts in 1930.
They have lifted the trophy twice - in 1930 and 1950.
United States: The USA reached the semi-finals of the first World Cup in 1930.
The USA co-hosts the 2026 World Cup with Canada and Mexico.
Japan: Japan co-hosted the 2002 World Cup with South Korea.
They have reached the World Cup knockout stage several times since 2002.
Switzerland: They have reached the knockout stage at several recent World Cups.
Iran: Iran have qualified for the World Cup six times.
Iran beat the United States 2-1 at the 1998 World Cup in France.
Turkey: Turkey finished third at the 2002 World Cup, their best result.
Hakan Şükür scored the fastest goal in World Cup history there - after 11 seconds.
Ecuador: Ecuador reached the Round of 16 at the 2006 World Cup.
They play home qualifiers in Quito, over 2,800m above sea level.
Austria: Austria finished third at the 1954 World Cup.
The 'Wunderteam' of the 1930s was one of Europe's strongest sides.
South Korea: South Korea reached the semi-finals as co-hosts in 2002 - the best by an Asian side.
They co-hosted the 2002 World Cup with Japan.
Australia: Australia switched from the Oceania confederation to Asia in 2006.
The Socceroos reached the Round of 16 in both 2006 and 2022.
Algeria: Algeria famously beat West Germany 2-1 at the 1982 World Cup.
They won the Africa Cup of Nations in 1990 and again in 2019.
Egypt: Egypt were the first African nation to enter the World Cup, in 1934.
They are record seven-time Africa Cup of Nations champions.
Canada: Canada's only previous men's World Cup appearance was in 1986.
Canada co-hosts the 2026 World Cup with the USA and Mexico.
Norway: Norway went unbeaten against Brazil in the 20th century, beating them at the 1998 World Cup.
Striker Erling Haaland is one of the most prolific goalscorers in the game.
Ivory Coast: Ivory Coast won the Africa Cup of Nations on home soil in 2024.
Didier Drogba is the nation's all-time leading scorer.
Panama: Panama made their World Cup debut in 2018.
Their 2017 qualification was declared a national holiday.
Sweden: Sweden finished runners-up at the 1958 World Cup, which they hosted.
They took third place at the 1994 World Cup in the United States.
Czechia: As Czechoslovakia they reached two World Cup finals, in 1934 and 1962.
Czechoslovakia won the 1976 European Championship on penalties.
Paraguay: Paraguay reached the World Cup quarter-finals in 2010.
Goalkeeper José Luis Chilavert scored dozens of goals from free-kicks and penalties.
Scotland: Scotland played in the first-ever international football match, against England in 1872.
Hampden Park in Glasgow is one of the world's oldest international stadiums.
Tunisia: Tunisia were the first African team to win a World Cup match, in 1978.
They won the Africa Cup of Nations in 2004 as hosts.
DR Congo: As Zaire, they were the first Black African nation to reach a World Cup, in 1974.
The nation has won the Africa Cup of Nations twice, in 1968 and 1974.
Uzbekistan: Uzbekistan won the football gold medal at the 1994 Asian Games.
The 'White Wolves' joined FIFA after independence in the early 1990s.
Qatar: Qatar hosted the 2022 World Cup, the first in the Middle East.
They won the AFC Asian Cup in both 2019 and 2023.
Iraq: Iraq won the AFC Asian Cup in 2007, uniting the country in celebration.
Their only previous World Cup appearance was in 1986.
South Africa: South Africa hosted the 2010 World Cup, the first held in Africa.
They won the Africa Cup of Nations as hosts in 1996.
Saudi Arabia: Saudi Arabia stunned Argentina 2-1 at the 2022 World Cup.
They reached the Round of 16 on their World Cup debut in 1994.
Jordan: Jordan reached their first-ever Asian Cup final in 2024.
That run was the nation's best result at a major tournament.
Bosnia and Herzegovina: Bosnia and Herzegovina made their World Cup debut in 2014.
Edin Džeko is the country's all-time leading scorer.
Cape Verde: Cape Verde, the 'Blue Sharks', are an Atlantic island nation of around half a million people.
They reached the Africa Cup of Nations quarter-finals in 2013 and again in 2023.
Ghana: Ghana reached the World Cup quarter-finals in 2010, agonisingly close to the semis.
The Black Stars are four-time Africa Cup of Nations champions.
Curaçao: Curaçao is a Caribbean island and constituent country of the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
They won the Caribbean Cup in 2017.
Haiti: Haiti's only previous World Cup appearance was in 1974.
There, Emmanuel Sanon ended Italy goalkeeper Dino Zoff's long unbeaten record.
New Zealand: New Zealand went unbeaten at the 2010 World Cup, drawing all three games.
The men's national team is nicknamed the 'All Whites'.
